body:
UNSW Business School
INFS2822 Programming for Data Analytics




Lecture 5
Data Exploration and Manipulation


Term 3, 2025
  Copyright
• There are some file-sharing websites that specialise in buying and selling
 academic work to and from university students.

• If you upload your original work to these websites, and if another student
 downloads and presents it as their own either wholly or partially, you
 might be found guilty of collusion — even years after graduation.

• These file-sharing websites may also accept purchase of course
 materials, such as copies of lecture slides and tutorial handouts. By law,
 the copyright on course materials, developed by UNSW staff in the
 course of their employment, belongs to UNSW. It constitutes copyright
 infringement, if not academic misconduct, to trade these materials.

 5
                                                                6
Reminders
• Individual assignment due by Week 5 Friday 17th October, 3
 pm AEST

• Group assignment will be out today
                                                                       7
“Guess the Number” Game
• Python picks a random number within a range
• Give the user some number of tries to guess the number
• After each guess, tell the user whether the guess is too high or
 too low
• User wins if the number is guessed within guess limit, and
 loses otherwise



                                                               CODE
                           8
Outline
• Data and Data Science

• Series

• DataFrame
                                                         9
A Motivating Example (1/2)
• Alice is looking for a new job (Data Analyst)
• What should be the appropriate salary
   ⎻ $2,000?
   ⎻ $5,000?
   ⎻ $2,000,000?
   ⎻ $10,000,000?

• The salary should not be too low or too high
• How could we know the appropriate salary for Alice?
                                                                       10
A Motivating Example (2/2)
• How could we know the appropriate salary for Alice?

• Data Analytics Consideration
   ⎻ Do we have salary data of other people?
   ⎻ What are the features (education, skills, years of experience,
     certificate)?
   ⎻ How can we use the data to predict?
                                                                                11
Data
• Data
       ⎻ “factual information (such as measurements or statistics) used as a
         basis for reasoning, discussion, or calculation”
• Structured data
       ⎻ With strict format (e.g., data stored in DB)
       ⎻ Not all data collected are structured
• Unstructured data
       ⎻ Very limited indication of data format
       ⎻ E.g., a simple text on a website, an article, social media messages


Source: https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/data
                                                      12
Data Science


“Data science is an interdisciplinary
field focused on extracting knowledge
from typically large data sets and
applying the knowledge and insights
from that data to solve problems in a
wide range of application domains.”




Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_science
                                                                 13
Libraries in Python
• Pre-written collections of code that provide additional
 functionality to your Python programs

• Simplify your coding process

• Contain a set of modules, functions, and classes

• Libraries act as toolboxes that contain pre-built tools for
 specific tasks
                                                                 14
Data Science Tools in Python
• Python toolboxes/libraries:
   ⎻ NumPy (manipulation of homogeneous array-based data)
   ⎻ Pandas (manipulation of heterogeneous and labeled data)
   ⎻ SciKit-Learn (machine learning)

• Visualization libraries
   ⎻ Matplotlib (publication-quality visualizations)
   ⎻ Seaborn (data visualization library based on matplotlib)
                                                                 15
NumPy (short for Numerical Python)
• Introduces objects for multidimensional arrays and matrices
• Provides functions that allow performing advanced
  mathematical and statistical operations
• Provides vectorization on arrays and matrices which
  significantly improves the performance
• Serves as a foundation for other python libraries such as
  SciPy (short for Scientific Python)



Source: https://numpy.org/
                                                                     16
Pandas
• Adds data structures and tools designed to work with table-like
  data (Series and Data Frames)
• Provides tools for data manipulation, e.g., reshaping, merging,
  sorting, slicing, aggregation, etc.




Source: https://pandas.pydata.org/
                                                           17
SciKit-Learn
• Provides machine learning algorithms: classification,
  regression, clustering, model validation, etc.
• Integrated use with NumPy, pandas and matplotlib




Source: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/
                                                                                     18
Matplotlib and Seaborn
• Matplotlib:
        ⎻ Is a Python 2D plotting library which produces publication quality
          figures
        ⎻ Provides functionalities similar to those of MATLAB (another
          programming language)
        ⎻ Provides line plots, scatter plots, bar charts, histograms, pie charts,
          etc.

• Seaborn:
        ⎻ Is based on Matplotlib
        ⎻ Provides a high-level interface for visualizing data

Source: https://matplotlib.org/; https://seaborn.pydata.org/
                           19
Outline
• Data and Data Science

• Series

• DataFrame
                                                                                            20
Pandas
• Offers data structures and operations for manipulating
  numerical tables and time series
• After importing the library with the below conventional short
  form pd, you can access its functions and classes to perform
  specific tasks

        import pandas as pd

Note: By default, pandas has been installed in Anaconda Distribution. If you do NOT have
pandas, please visit How to Install Pandas in Python?
                                                       21
Data in Pandas
• We use pandas for
        ⎻ Series data (1d)
        ⎻ DataFrame data (2d)
        ⎻ Panel (time series 3d)

• Data set for this lecture
        ⎻ Employee data1
        ⎻ Available on Moodle



1 https://liud22.oss-us-east-1.aliyuncs.com/emp.csv
                                                        22
Series
• A Series is a one-dimensional array-like object
 containing an array of data (of any NumPy data
 type) and an associated array of data labels,
 called its index
• A pandas Series can hold data of various types
 (true/false, integers, strings, floating numbers,
 objects, etc.)
• By default, when you create a Series without
 specifying a data type, pandas will attempt to
 infer the data type based on the values you
 provide. If the values in the Series are of
 different types, pandas will assign the data type
 that can accommodate all the different types,
 resulting in a Series with a data type of "object".
• We can create a Series from a list or a
 dictionary
                                                                    23
Creating Series from List

import pandas as pd
our_list = [3, -5, 7, 4]
s1 = pd.Series(our_list, index=['num1', 'num2', 'num3', 'num4'])
print(s1) # Series is basically a one-dimensional labeled array
                                                          24
Creating Series from Dictionary (1/2)


price_data = {'2019': 1.85, '2020': 1.70} # dictionary
print(price_data)
s2 = pd.Series(price_data )
print(s2)
                                                          25
Creating Series from Dictionary (2/2)


price_data = {'2019': 1.85, '2020': 1.70} # dictionary

# Reordering the elements when creating Series
s3 = pd.Series(price_data, index = ['2020', '2019' ])
print(s3)
                                                                    26
View the Index of a Series


our_list = [3, -5, 7, 4]
s1 = pd.Series(our_list, index=['num1', 'num2', 'num3', 'num4'])
print(s1.index)
print(s1.values)
                                                                27
Exercise
• Reordering the dictionary elements when creating a Pandas
 Series (in the series, have weight first and then height).

 dicdata = {'height': 1.85, 'weight': 170}      # dictionary
 print(dicdata)

 #your code should be
 s4 = pd.Series (dicdata, index = ['weight', 'height'])
 print(s4)
                                                             28
Initiating Values in a Series
• Creating a Pandas Series, initiating all values as 0:
      s5 = pd.Series(0, index = range(10)) #0 is a scalar
      print(s5)
                                                                             29
Slice Series
• A Pandas Series has indexing. We can "slice" a Pandas series
 by index or position. For example, we can return a single
 element in a Pandas series by index or position.
  print(s1)
  s1[0] # returns the 1st element (position); deprecated soon
  s1[3] # returns the 4th element (position); deprecated soon
  s1["num1"] # returns the element with index "num1"


• Return a group of elements               Index Label   Value   Position

  s1[:2]                                   num1            3         0

  s1[:"num3"] # the first three elements   num2            -5        1
                                           num3            7         2
                                           num4            4         3
                                                         s1
 Accessing Elements in a Redefined                                                      30

Order
• Elements are in order
                                                      Index Label   Value   Position
• We can print elements in a redefined order          num1            3        0
  print(s1[[2,0,1]])                                  num2            -5       1
                                                      num3            7        2
                                                      num4            4        3




• Slicing series using index label (access series like a dictionary)
  s1["num1"]      # returns the element with index LABEL "num1"
                                                                 31
Describe the Data in Series
• Describe statistics of a series of numbers using describe()
• describe() function generate a descriptive statistics that
  summarize the central tendency, dispersion, and shape of a
  dataset's distribution for the given series object
• All the calculations are performed by excluding NaN values
  (i.e., missing or undefined data)
  print(s1.median())
  print(s1.describe())
  print(s1.describe().apply("{0:.3f}".format))
  #3 decimal places; formatted as a string type
                                                               32
Aggregation
• Aggregate functions can be used on series:
  ⎻ count() – Number of non-null observations
  ⎻ sum() – Sum of values
  ⎻ mean() – Mean of values
  ⎻ median() – Arithmetic median of values
  ⎻ min() – Minimum
  ⎻ max() – Maximum
  ⎻ mode() – Mode, i.e., the most frequently appeared value
  ⎻ std() – Standard deviation
  ⎻ var() – Variance
                                                                  33
Exercise
• Create a series of your choice of numbers
• Print max and min
• Print all numbers larger than 0.6
• Print the first 5 numbers
• Print the last 5 numbers
                    our_nums = [0.1,0.3,0.5,0.7,0.9,0.11,0.13]
                    s_our_nums = pd.Series(our_nums)
                    print(s_our_nums.min())
                    print(s_our_nums.max())

                    print(s_our_nums[s_our_nums.values > 0.6])
                    print(s_our_nums.values[0:5])
                    print(s_our_nums.values[-5:])
 34
                           35
Outline
• Data and Data Science

• Series

• DataFrame
                                                               36
Pandas DataFrame
• DataFrame is two-dimensional, size-mutable data structure
  with rows and columns/a dictionary of Series objects.
• We can create a DataFrame using series and dictionary.
   professor = pd.Series(['Jeff','DP','Thomas'])
   age = pd.Series([50, 30, 25])
   df1 = pd.DataFrame({'Professor': professor, 'Age': age})
   df1
                                                           37
Add a New Column
• We can add new columns to the existing DataFrame in
  Pandas.
• Add a new column, e.g.,
   df1['years of experience'] = pd.Series([40,6.0,5.6])
   df1
                                                    38
Create DataFrame from Files
We can read a local/online file into a DataFrame
     import pandas as pd
     df2 = pd.read_csv("emp.csv")


OR
     df2 = pd.read_csv(”URL")
                                                                    39
Display Data Records
• head() function returns the first n rows from the DataFrame
 based on position. It is useful for quickly verifying data, for
 example, after sorting or appending rows.
  ⎻ E.g., display the first three records
     df2.head(3)


• tail() function returns the last n rows from the DataFrame
 based on position.
  ⎻ E.g., display the last three records:
     df2.tail(3)
                                                                             40
Read Data with Specified Index
• Read csv file from a file, with index_col indicating the
   column used for index
    import pandas as pd
    pd.read_csv("emp.csv", index_col="ID")


• index_col can be assigned to a column name or an integer
• (optional) It can also be assigned to a sequence of column
   names or integers for multi-level index


Source: https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html
                                                    41
Exercise
• Display first 10 records
• Display first 25 records
• Display the last 10 records (hint: df2.tail())

    df2.head(10)
    df2.head(25)
    df2.tail(10)
                                         42
Data Type in Pandas
• Identify the type of df object
      type(df2)


• Check the type of a column "salary"
      df2['salary'].dtype


• List the types of all columns
      df2.dtypes
                                                                                              43
Data Type in Pandas
Pandas Type    Native Python Type   Description
object         string               The most general dtype. Will be assigned to the column
                                    if column has mixed types (numbers and strings)

int64          int                  Numeric characters. 64 refers to the memory allocated

float64        float                Numeric characters with decimals. If a column contains
                                    numbers and NaNs (see the link below), pandas will
                                    default to float64
datetime64     N/A                  Values meant to hold time data



More Pandas Data Types: https://pbpython.com/pandas_dtypes.html
                                                               44
Columns & Axes
• Pandas DataFrame is a two-dimensional data structure with
  labeled axes (rows and columns). We can return the column
  labels of a given DataFrame.
• List the column names
     import pandas as pd
     df = pd.read_csv("emp.csv", index_col="ID")
     df.ndim #number of dimensions is 2
     df.columns
     #df.columns() is wrong
• List the axes labels (both row axis and column axis)
     df.axes
                                                     45
Useful Attributes and Functions
• Number of dimensions
 df.ndim

• Total number of elements in the Data Frame
 df.size

• Number of rows and columns
 df.shape

• Output basic statistics for the numeric columns
 df.describe()
                                                                   46
Mean/Max/Min/Median
• Pandas dataframe.mean() method returns the average
 (mean) value for numeric columns (column slicing is in the
 next few slides). We can calculate mean values for our defined
 df as follows:
     df.mean(numeric_only=True)
     #without the param setting, df.mean() generates error
     df[col selection of numeric columns].mean()
     #we can only apply mean() to numeric columns
• Other methods
     df.max()
     df.min()
     df.median()
                                                                   47
Slicing by Column
• The most basic way to select a single column from a DataFrame
 is using the dictionary mechanism, df[column name](the column
 name is really a key in the dictionary)

• E.g., we can return the column called gender
 df['Gender']
 #df['gender'] is wrong, column names are case sensitive
                                                           48
Slicing by Location
We can also use .loc[] to select both rows and columns,
based on index labels and column names

     df_exercise = pd.read_csv('emp.csv')
     df_exercise.loc[10:20, ['position', 'salary']]
                                                                                49
.loc[] Property
• The .loc[] property is used to access a group of rows and
  columns, and is primarily label-based, i.e., not using position.
• When using .loc[row selection, col selection], the
  allowed inputs for row selection and col selection are:
    ⎻ A single row index label or column name, e.g., 5 or 'a'
    ⎻ A list or array of index labels or column names, e.g., ['a', 'b', 'c']
    ⎻ A range of index labels or column names, e.g., 'a':'f' (the ending
      label 'f' is included)
    ⎻ A Boolean array of the same length as the axis (row or column
      axis) being sliced, e.g. [True, False, True]
• Note: row selection is mandatory, col selection is optional.
 Hence when we want all rows, use ‘:’ for row selection.
                                                                 50
.loc[] Example (1/3)
If we want to select both rows and columns, we can use .loc[]

   import pandas as pd
   df_exercise = pd.read_csv("emp.csv")
   df_exercise.loc[64:67, ['position', 'salary']]
                          51
.loc[] Example (2/3)
   df_exercise.loc[64]
                                                                      52
.loc[] Example (3/3)
df_exercise.loc[:,"position"] #row selection is ":", i.e. all rows
                                                                 53
Filtering
• We can use the logical operators on column values to filter
  rows in DataFrame
• Print data with salary > 100,000
     df_sub = df[df['salary'] > 100000]
     df_sub
                                                                      54
Filtering
• Filtering female assistant professors in two steps
df_female = df[df['Gender'] == 'Female']
df_result = df_female[df_female['position']=='AssistantProfessor']
print(df_result.count())
                                                                           55
Multiple Filters in One Step
• Filtering female assistant professors in one step using
 element-wise operator, & (we CANNOT use ‘and’)
  df[(df['Gender'] == 'Female')&(df['position']=='AssistantProfessor')]


• Filtering star employees with either salary above $150K or
 impact factor above 50, in one step using element-wise
 operator, | (we CANNOT use ‘or’)
  df[(df['salary'] > 150000) | (df['impact factor'] > 50)]
                                                                            56
Exercise
• Filtering: List the average salary for the IS discipline
 (Information Systems), having it rounded to three decimal
 places.

 df[df['discipline'] =='Information Systems'] ['salary'].mean().round(3)
 #round(3) function can round a number to three digits
                                                                             57
Sorting
• Sort the data frame by a column and create a new frame for
 assignment (default is ascending order)

  df_sorted = df.sort_values(by = 'years of experience')
  df_sorted.head()


• Sort the data by a column in descending order and overwrite the
 original dataset using inplace parameter

  #Sort the data by a column and overwrite the original dataset
  df.sort_values(by = 'impact factor', ascending = False, inplace = True)
  df.head()
                                                                                                       58
Restore the Initial Order
We can sort by index in ascending order to restore the initial order

        df.sort_index(axis=0, ascending = True, inplace = True)
        df.head()



                                                              inplace bool, default False: Whether
 axis{0 or ‘index’, 1 or ‘columns’}, default 0                to modify the DataFrame in the
                                                              original place rather than creating a
                                                              new one
                             ascending bool or list-like of bools, default True
                                                  59
Sorting
• Sort the data frame using 2 or more columns:
  df_sorted = df.sort_values(
      by = ['years of experience', 'salary'],
      ascending = [False,False])
  df_sorted.head(10)
                                                                 60
Grouping (1/2)
• We can perform an aggregate calculation (e.g., average, or
 frequency count) on a variable in DataFrame, e.g., calculate
 average salary for all people
     df['salary'].mean()
• We can also group data and perform an aggregate calculation
 for each group, e.g., we can group data based on position
 and calculate the average salary of each position
     df_position = df.groupby('position')
     df_position['salary'].mean()
                                                                 61
Grouping (2/2)
• Calculate the average salary for each gender (single square
 brackets around salary)
     df.groupby('Gender')['salary'].mean()


• If we use double square brackets Pandas will produce a
 DataFrame
     df.groupby('Gender')[['salary']].mean()
                                                                                      62
  Grouping by 2 Variables
  • We can group data by more than one variable
  • E.g., we can group the emp data by two variables position
      and gender:
          df.groupby(['position', 'Gender'])['salary'].mean()




Source: https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.groupby.html
                                                                 63
Exercise
• Group data by discipline and display the average salary for
 each group

  df.groupby('discipline')['salary'].mean()
                                                                  64
Summary of DataFrame’s Attributes
 df.attribute   Description

 dtypes         list the types of the columns
 columns        list the column names
 axes           list the row labels and column names
 ndim           number of dimensions
 size           number of elements
 shape          return a tuple representing the dimensionality
 values         NumPy representation of the data
                                                                     65
Summary of DataFrame’s Methods
 df.method()        description
 head(n), tail(n)   first/last n rows
 describe()         generate descriptive statistics (for numeric
                    columns only)
 max(), min()       return max/min values for all numeric columns
 mean(), median()   return mean/median values for all numeric
                    columns
 std()              standard deviation
 sample(n)          returns a random sample with n rows


 Unlike attributes, Python methods have parenthesis ( )
             66
Questions
